ExecutionLogging.^description: API Gateway REST or WebSocket API stages should have relevant logs enabled. API Gateway REST and WebSocket API execution logging provides detailed records of requests made to API Gateway REST and WebSocket API stages. The stages include API integration backend responses, Lambda authorizer responses, and the requestId for AWS integration endpoints.
ref: [Set up CloudWatch API logging using the API Gateway console]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/set-up-logging.html#set-up-access-logging-using-console>

EncryptionInTransit.^description: API Gateway REST API stages should be configured with SSL certificates to allow backend systems to authenticate that requests originate from API Gateway.
ref: [Generate and configure an SSL certificate for backend authentication]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/getting-started-client-side-ssl-authentication.html>

XRayTracing.^description: X-Ray active tracing enables a more rapid response to performance changes in the underlying infrastructure. Changes in performance could result in a lack of availability of the API. X-Ray active tracing provides real-time metrics of user requests that flow through your API Gateway REST API operations and connected services.
ref: [Amazon API Gateway active tracing support for AWS X-Ray]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/xray/latest/devguide/xray-services-apigateway.html>

WAFWACL.^description: AWS WAF is a web application firewall that helps protect web applications and APIs from attacks. It enables you to configure an ACL, which is a set of rules that allow, block, or count web requests based on customizable web security rules and conditions that you define. Ensure that your API Gateway stage is associated with an AWS WAF web ACL to help protect it from malicious attacks.
ref: [API Gateway should be associated with a WAF Web ACL]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/apigateway-control-access-aws-waf.html>

CachingEnabled.^description: You can enable API caching in Amazon API Gateway to cache your endpoint's responses. With caching, you can reduce the number of calls made to your endpoint and also improve the latency of requests to your API.
ref: [Cache settings for REST APIs in API Gateway]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/api-gateway-caching.html>

EncryptionAtRest.^description: Encrypting data at rest reduces the risk of data stored on disk being accessed by a user not authenticated to AWS. It adds another set of access controls to limit unauthorized users ability access the data. For example, API permissions are required to decrypt the data before it can be read. API Gateway REST API caches should be encrypted at rest for an added layer of security.
ref: [Configuring encryption at rest for API Gateway cache data]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/api-gateway-caching.html#api-gateway-caching-encryption>

AuthorizationType.^description: API Gateway V2 WebSocket and HTTP routes should specify an authorization type. Specifying an authorization type ensures that access to the API is controlled and restricted to authorized users or processes.
ref: [Configuring authorization for API Gateway V2 WebSocket APIs]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/apigateway-websocket-api-authorization.html>
[Configuring authorization for API Gateway V2 HTTP APIs]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/http-api-auth-providers.html>

AccessLogging.^description: API Gateway access logs provide detailed information about who has accessed your API and how the caller accessed the API. These logs are useful for applications such as security and access audits and forensics investigation. Enable these access logs to analyze traffic patterns and to troubleshoot issues.

MinTLSVersion.^description: API Gateway REST API domains should use a security policy that specifies a minimum TLS protocol version of TLSv1.2. Using a minimum TLS version of 1.2 helps ensure that secure communication is established between the client and the API Gateway.
MinTLSVersion.shortDesc: API Gateway REST API domains should use a security policy with a minimum TLS version of TLSv1.2
ref: [Configuring a minimum TLS version for API Gateway REST API domains]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/security-policy.html>

IdleAPIGateway.^description: Info only: {$COUNT} APIGateway(s) is not deployed
ref: [Setting up a stage using the API Gateway console]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/stages.html>

ResourcePolicy.^description: API Gateway REST APIs should have resource policies configured for access control. Resource policies allow you to control who can invoke your API based on IP addresses, VPC endpoints, AWS accounts, or other conditions. This provides an additional layer of security beyond IAM and authorization.
ResourcePolicy.shortDesc: Configure resource policies for access control
ref: [Control and manage access to REST APIs in API Gateway]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/apigateway-control-access-to-api.html>

IAMAuthentication.^description: API Gateway methods should use proper authentication instead of NONE authorization. Methods with NONE authorization allow unauthenticated access, which poses a security risk. Use IAM authentication, Lambda authorizers, or Cognito user pools to control access to your API methods.
IAMAuthentication.shortDesc: Use IAM or other authentication for API methods
ref: [Control and manage access to REST APIs in API Gateway]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/apigateway-control-access-to-api.html>

RequestThrottling.^description: API Gateway stages should have throttling limits configured to protect from traffic spikes and prevent backend system overload. Throttling helps improve overall throughput and stability by setting rate and burst limits at the stage or method level.

PrivateAPI.^description: Private APIs should be properly configured with VPC endpoints for secure access. Private APIs are accessible only from within your VPC using interface VPC endpoints, isolating traffic from the public internet. Ensure private APIs have VPC endpoint IDs configured.
PrivateAPI.shortDesc: Configure private APIs with VPC endpoints

LambdaAuthorizer.^description: Lambda authorizers enable custom authorization logic for API Gateway. They support bearer token authentication and request parameter-based authorization decisions. Verify that Lambda authorizers are properly configured and used by API methods when custom authorization is needed.
LambdaAuthorizer.shortDesc: Configure Lambda authorizers for custom authorization

CognitoAuthorizer.^description: Amazon Cognito user pools provide authentication for API Gateway. They enable you to create customizable authentication solutions and control who can invoke API methods. Verify that Cognito authorizers are properly configured when user authentication is required.
CognitoAuthorizer.shortDesc: Configure Cognito user pools for authentication
ref: [Control access to REST APIs using Amazon Cognito user pools]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/apigateway-integrate-with-cognito.html>

MutualTLS.^description: Mutual TLS authentication provides two-way authentication between clients and API Gateway. It verifies client certificates to enhance security for sensitive APIs. Enable mutual TLS for custom domains to ensure both client and server authentication.
MutualTLS.shortDesc: Enable mutual TLS for custom domains

UsagePlanThrottling.^description: Usage plans should have throttling and quota limits configured to track and limit API usage per API key. Set appropriate rate limits, burst limits, and quota limits to manage API consumption and prevent abuse. Note that usage plan quotas are best-effort and not hard limits.
UsagePlanThrottling.shortDesc: Configure throttling and quotas in usage plans
ref: [Usage plans and API keys for REST APIs]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/api-gateway-api-usage-plans.html>

VPCEndpointAssociation.^description: Private APIs must be associated with VPC endpoints to enable secure access from within your VPC. Verify that private APIs have VPC endpoint IDs configured and that the endpoints exist. This ensures proper network isolation and secure connectivity.
VPCEndpointAssociation.shortDesc: Associate private APIs with VPC endpoints

RequestValidation.^description: Request validators should be configured to validate request parameters and body before passing requests to backend integrations. This reduces invalid requests reaching your backend, improves API security and reliability, and provides clear error messages for validation failures.

LeastPrivilegeAccess.^description: API Gateway should have appropriate access controls configured including resource policies and proper authorization. Verify that resource policies exist, authorization types are not NONE, and at least one authorizer is configured. This ensures comprehensive access control across multiple layers.

CloudWatchAlarms.^description: CloudWatch alarms should be configured for key API Gateway metrics including 4XXError, 5XXError, Latency, and Count. Alarms enable proactive monitoring and rapid response to API issues. Verify that alarms exist for critical metrics and have appropriate actions configured.
CloudWatchAlarms.shortDesc: Configure CloudWatch alarms for API metrics

APIKeyAntiPattern.^description: API keys should not be used as the sole authentication mechanism. API keys are intended for usage tracking and rate limiting, not authentication. Methods that require API keys should also have proper authorization configured (IAM, Lambda authorizer, or Cognito). Flag methods that rely only on API keys.
APIKeyAntiPattern.shortDesc: Avoid using API keys for authentication

VPCAccessRestrictions.^description: Private APIs should have resource policies with VPC-specific conditions to restrict access. Verify that resource policies include aws:SourceVpc or aws:SourceVpce conditions to ensure only authorized VPCs can access the API. This provides network-level isolation for private APIs.
VPCAccessRestrictions.shortDesc: Configure VPC access restrictions for private APIs

PerformanceMonitoring.^description: CloudWatch dashboards and alarms should be configured for API performance monitoring. Verify that dashboards exist for visualizing API metrics and that performance-related alarms are configured. This enables proactive performance management and rapid issue detection.

CanaryDeployment.^description: Canary deployments enable gradual rollout of API changes by routing a percentage of traffic to new versions. This reduces risk by allowing validation with production traffic before full deployment. Verify that canary settings are configured for production stages.
CanaryDeployment.shortDesc: Configure canary deployments for gradual rollouts

StageVariables.^description: Stage variables enable environment-specific configuration without code changes. They can be used to specify backend endpoints, Lambda function versions, or other environment-specific values. Using stage variables improves deployment flexibility and reduces configuration errors.
StageVariables.shortDesc: Use stage variables for environment configuration

StageLifecycle.^description: Proper stage lifecycle management includes maintaining multiple stages (dev, test, prod) with appropriate naming conventions and configurations. Multiple stages enable safe testing and gradual promotion of changes. Verify that APIs follow stage management best practices.

CORSConfiguration.^description: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) configuration is required for APIs accessed from web browsers on different domains. Proper CORS configuration prevents security issues while enabling legitimate cross-origin requests. Verify that CORS is configured when needed for browser-based clients.
